synopsis: The CJB investigated serious procedural failures by Southern Florida Presbytery and its Judicial Commission in handling the discipline of TE Phil Maddux over a four-year period (1983-1987) involving divorce, suspension from office, and excommunication. The Presbytery ultimately rescinded its actions and restored Maddux on April 20, 1987, rendering further GA action moot, but the CJB reported specific failures for the edification of all presbyteries.

title: Report on North Texas Presbytery and North Dallas Presbyterian Church

synopsis: The CJB investigated the actions of North Texas Presbytery regarding North Dallas Presbyterian Church following referral by the 14th GA, finding sixteen specific procedural exceptions where Presbytery failed to follow BCO and RAO requirements. The CJB found no evidence of bad faith but concluded that most injustices were irreversible due to elapsed time, recommending that improper censures against two ruling elders be acknowledged and that First Presbyterian Church of Rowlette be free to pursue civil action to recover property.

title: Constitutional Inquiry #1 from Central Carolina Presbytery (extraordinary clause for ordination)
synopsis: Central Carolina Presbytery inquired about the scope of the extraordinary clause in BCO 18-2 for ordination, including whether presbytery may waive the six-month endorsement, the two-month filing deadline, the internship sequence, and whether college and seminary diplomas are pre-requisites versus trial parts. The committee answered each sub-question, concluding that the three educational pre-requisites in BCO 21-4 are not parts of the trial for ordination and cannot be waived under the extraordinary clause.

title: Chappell v. Eastern Carolina Presbytery
topics: suspension of session without due process
synopsis: Ruling Elder Chappell complained against Eastern Carolina Presbytery's Commission, which had suspended the session of Fuller Memorial Presbyterian Church and constituted itself as a 'Session Pro Tem' without due process or congregational consent. The SJC sustained the complaint, holding that the BCO grants no authority for a presbytery to depose a session and act in its place, that suspension requires due process, and that the congregation has an inalienable right to be governed by officers it has chosen.

title: Melton et al. v. Tennessee Valley Presbytery

parties.complainant_or_appellant: Bryan Melton, Mrs. Bryan Melton, and Mrs. JoAnn Davis

topics: higher court authority over local church
synopsis: Complainants challenged the ruling of the session of Westminster Presbyterian Church (Chattanooga) disallowing proxy votes at a December 10, 1989 congregational meeting that voted not to rescind the earlier dissolution and property transfer to New City Fellowship. The SJC denied all specifications, holding that proxy voting was not required under BCO 24-4 or Robert's Rules, and that the congregation's decision to dissolve was within its rights under BCO Chapter 25.
description: The essential facts of this case, pertinent to this complaint, are not in dispute. title: Stringer v. Mississippi Valley Presbytery
synopsis: Randy Stringer appealed his excommunication by Mississippi Valley Presbytery after being deposed and then excommunicated for failure to respond to the presbytery. The SJC set aside the excommunication and remanded the case to MVP, holding that after deposition the presbytery was required under BCO 46-8 to assign Stringer to membership in a particular church rather than proceed to excommunication. Dr. Morton H. Smith dissented, arguing that the presbytery had proper grounds to proceed to excommunication after Stringer's contumacy.
description: On June 2, 1989, Mississippi Valley Presbytery (MVP) tried and convicted Randy C. stringer on a number of charges. mvp inflicted the censures of deposition and indefinite suspension from the Sacraments on Mr. Stringer. canonical_title: Stringer v. Mississippi Valley Presbytery

title: Bowen v. Eastern Carolina Presbytery
topics: fundamentals of the system of doctrine
suspension from office without due process
synopsis: TE David Bowen complained against Eastern Carolina Presbytery's actions rescinding its 1988 decision and annulling the elections and ordinations of ruling elders and deacons at the Church of the Good Shepherd who took exception to infant baptism and limited atonement. The SJC sustained the complaint that ECP erred in rescinding a two-year-old action and annulling ordinations without due process under BCO 24-6, while also affirming that infant baptism and limited atonement are fundamentals of the system of doctrine for which no exceptions may be granted for church officers.

title: Hathaway v. Grace Presbytery
synopsis: TE Vaughn Hathaway complained that the Session of Waynesboro Presbyterian Church failed to institute discipline against seven members who had not attended worship for over a year. The judicial panel denied the complaint on the grounds that the Session retained discretion to determine the timing of discipline proceedings, and procedural errors did not result in material prejudice.

title: Nickoley and Stephens v. Southwest Presbytery
synopsis: Bruce Nickoley and David Stephens complained against Southwest Presbytery's actions in withdrawing charges against two ruling elders, dissolving a pastoral relationship, and rescinding its suspension of a local church session. The SJC denied the complaint, affirming that the Presbytery correctly determined it lacked constitutional authority to suspend the session or act as a 'Session pro tem' without congregational consent, and that original jurisdiction over charges against ruling elders rested with the local session.

title: Antioch Session v. Eastern Carolina Presbytery
synopsis: Antioch Session complained that Eastern Carolina Presbytery erred in licensing Arthur Peterson given his divorce/remarriage history and his views on creation and a local flood. The SJC did not sustain the complaint on Issue 1 (licensure approval, 13-4), finding ECP acted with constitutional thoroughness and that precedent permitted a divorced man to hold office under certain conditions, but did sustain Issue 2 (3-0 panel; 17-0 SJC), finding ECP negligent in allowing Peterson to serve as stated supply for over six months before licensing him.

canonical_title: Session of Third Presbyterian v. Evangel — M31GA, 2003 Charlotte, p. 101. JOO 19-0 Summary: Lease dispute between church and school (separate corporations). title: Deacon Neil Jeansonne v. Eastern Carolina Presbytery
synopsis: In light of the SJC's decision in Case 2001-33, the April 21, 2001 ECP judgment reversing Jeansonne's conviction stands, and the case was remanded to the Session of Christ Presbyterian Church. BCO 43-1 prohibits complaints in judicial cases where an appeal has been taken.
description: 1. On July 19, 2000 the Session of Christ Presbyterian Church (CPC) in Greenville, North Carolina found deacon Neil Jeansonne guilty of the “public offense of causing dissension and strife among the brethren” and imposed the censures of deposition from the office of deacon and indefinite suspension from the sacrament of the Lord’s Supper.

title: Christ Covenant Church Session v. Central Carolina Presbytery
synopsis: The SJC ruled the case was not judicially in order because the Session of Christ Covenant Church should not have received the original complaint, which was filed more than 30 days after the verdict was received, exceeding the time limit under BCO 43-2. The underlying dispute concerned a session trial commission's acquittal of a member charged with abandonment and seeking an unbiblical divorce.
description: 1. On November 22, 1997 Joel Boyles (hereafter referred to as H) and Kasey Crowell (hereafter referred as W) were married. 2. On April 3, 1998 H & W were interviewed for membership in Christ Covenant Church (CCC), Matthews, NC and, while the record is not clear, presumably they were admitted into membership of the church at or about that date.

title: TE W.W. Marshall Jr. v. Eastern Carolina Presbytery
synopsis: The SJC sustained the complaint, finding that ECP erred in hearing and acting on RE Williams' complaint on July 21, 2001 because BCO 43-1 prohibits complaints in judicial cases where an appeal has been taken. The word 'taken' refers to any appeal whether past, current, or pending, and ECP's action rescinding its prior judgment was therefore improper.

title: RE Sang Bai Kim and RE Chan Soo Kim v. Korean Eastern Presbytery

parties.complainant_or_appellant: RE Sang Bai Kim and RE Chan Soo Kim

synopsis: The Korean Eastern Presbytery's Administrative Commission exceeded its authority under BCO 13-9 by calling and facilitating a congregational meeting to vote on ruling elders without proper BCO authority, as complaint procedures under BCO 25-2 and 43 should have been followed instead. The SJC sustained the complaint, reaffirming precedents limiting presbytery power and the principle that a higher court may not 'act for' a lower court.

canonical_title: Sang B. Kim and Chan S. Kim v. Korean Eastern — M31GA, 2003 Charlotte, p. 194. Sustained 15-3 Summary: Alleged P Commission improperly called congregational meeting to vote on REs. title: RE Sang Bai Kim and RE Chan Soo Kim v. Korean Eastern Presbytery

parties.complainant_or_appellant: RE Sang Bai Kim and RE Chan Soo Kim

synopsis: The SJC affirmed KEP's denial of the appeal regarding the underlying guilty verdicts, but sustained the error of increasing the censure from suspension to excommunication and deposition on appeal, holding that appellate courts may not increase censures beyond what the trial court imposed. The case arose from discipline of two ruling elders who refused to recognize the results of a congregational election.

title: Carolyn Goerig v. Pacific Northwest Presbytery
synopsis: The SJC sustained the complaint, finding that the Session of Faith Presbyterian Church failed to follow BCO 38-4 procedures when it attempted to retroactively erase Carolyn Goerig's membership after banning her from attending services, and that the Presbytery erred in denying her complaint. Because her membership transfer to another church was never completed, she remained under Faith PC's jurisdiction per BCO 46-3.

canonical_title: Goerig v. Pacific Northwest Presbytery — M31GA, 2003 Charlotte, p. 116. Sustained 17-0 Summary: S removed name from roll per BCO 38-4. title: TE Brian Abshire v. Pacific Northwest Presbytery
synopsis: The SJC largely denied TE Abshire's complaint against Pacific Northwest Presbytery, finding that the Presbytery did not err in appointing a judicial commission or issuing a pastoral exhortation, but sustained the error in the commission's ruling on Lynch and Rooney's complaint without following BCO 43 procedures, remanding that specific matter to Presbytery.

Two members of the congregation then filed charges against those two REs. The pastor and other RE on Session administratively suspended the two REs from office.

title: Lachman v. Philadelphia Presbytery

synopsis: RE Lachman appealed his conviction by the Session of Calvary Presbyterian Church on charges of violating the fifth, sixth, and ninth commandments through contentious correspondence and conduct, arguing the prosecution relied solely on documentary evidence without calling witnesses. The SJC denied the appeal, holding that documentary evidence combined with the appellant's acknowledgment of the letters constituted sufficient witness testimony; a concurring opinion recommended that Sessions facing strained relations with an accused elder consider referring such cases to Presbytery.

title: Session of Delhi Presbyterian Church v. Louisiana Presbytery
synopsis: The Session of Delhi Presbyterian Church complained that Louisiana Presbytery improperly assumed original jurisdiction over church member Mike Holland, barring him from the Lord's Table for contumacy after he was cited in SPLC reporting regarding Auburn Avenue Presbyterian Church. The SJC sustained the complaint, holding that because Delhi Session had investigated and acted under BCO 31-2, Louisiana Presbytery lacked grounds to assume original jurisdiction under BCO 33-1, rendering its judgment against Holland a nullity; three dissenting opinions argued the SJC lacked appellate review of the jurisdiction question because no timely complaint was filed against the assumption of jurisdiction itself.

canonical_title: Session of Delhi PCA v. Louisiana — M32GA, 2004 Pittsburgh, p. 78. Sustained 18-3. Three D-Ops. title: Plowman v. Philadelphia Presbytery
synopsis: Mrs. Plowman was suspended from the Lord's Table and found guilty of contumacy by the Session of Lehigh Valley Presbyterian Church after refusing to compel her minor daughters to testify; her subsequent appeal was informally rejected by the chairman of Presbytery's Judicial Business Committee, who advised her to refile as a complaint. The SJC sustained both issues, holding that Mrs. Plowman had submitted to regular trial and had a right to appeal, and remanded the case to Philadelphia Presbytery for adjudication.

title: Merriam v. Tennessee Valley Presbytery

parties.complainant_or_appellant: RE Leland Nichols and RE Sam Couch

synopsis: Three combined judicial cases (2001-34, 2002-02, 2002-03) arising from West End Presbyterian Church's attempts to dissolve its pastoral relationship with TE Robert Wilson; James River Presbytery repeatedly delayed or refused to grant the congregation's constitutionally-made request. The SJC sustained the complaints (13-3), holding that JRP had no constitutional basis to delay granting the congregation's properly voted request to dissolve the pastoral relationship, though several of the 17 individual complaints were ruled judicially out of order.

title: Thornton v. Westminster Presbytery
synopsis: TE James Thornton complained that Westminster Presbytery erred by declining to place a valid call from Memorial PCA into his hands and simultaneously invoking BCO 34-10 to begin divestiture proceedings. The SJC sustained the complaint, holding that the administrative remedy of BCO 34-10 cannot be invoked when a call is already in the presbytery's possession, and that presbytery's refusal lacked objective, justifiable grounds required for appellate review.

synopsis: TE Michael Chastain appealed his conviction and indefinite suspension by Heritage Presbytery for divisive behavior, arising from his public letters to his congregation disagreeing with Session actions regarding an associate pastor call and a related Christian school controversy. The SJC denied the appeal (20-1), upholding the conviction and censure while finding procedural errors that did not rise to reversible level; a dissent by RE Frederick Neikirk argued the charge of 'divisive behavior' failed BCO 29-1's requirement that offenses be proven from Scripture.

The opinion affirms that PCA church courts have no authority to use civil courts to enforce ecclesiastical decisions, and that the PCA is a non-hierarchical denomination whose authority is moral and spiritual, not civil or coercive.

title: Brown v. Northern California Presbytery
synopsis: Northern California Presbytery adopted an abstract resolution declaring six different views on the diaconate permissible for ministers and Sessions; the SJC sustained the complaints and annulled the resolution, holding that no court may issue binding abstract decrees beyond its proper jurisdictional exercise. A concurring opinion analyzed each of the six diaconal views in detail and set out standards (the GUMP/Kniseley framework) for when diaconal assistant practices are constitutionally permissible.

Scott to the office of Teaching Elder by a vote of 19-17, which the SJC determined did not satisfy the BCO 34-8 requirement that 'the general sentiment of the Church is strongly in his favor.' The SJC annulled NFP's actions and remanded the case; a lengthy dissent by RE Donahoe argued that BCO 34-8 does not require a super-majority and that the restoration vote is distinct from the evaluation of general sentiment.

title: Hedman v. Pacific Northwest Presbytery
synopsis: RE Gerald Hedman complained that Pacific Northwest Presbytery unconstitutionally adopted five not-guilty verdicts regarding TE Peter Leithart's teachings on baptism, covenants, imputation, justification, and union with Christ. The SJC denied the complaint, finding it was bound by the Record of the Case and that Complainant had not demonstrated clear error on the part of Presbytery, with concurring and dissenting opinions discussing the scope of review, standing, and concerns about Leithart's sacramental theology.

synopsis: RE Reese and TE Bech complained that Philadelphia Presbytery erred in declaring their complaint out of order after Presbytery refused to allow any questions of TE Jason Hsu regarding his views on women deacons when approving his transfer to City Line Church. The SJC sustained the complaint, finding that deeming parts of a complaint objectionable is insufficient grounds to rule the whole complaint out of order, though it agreed the proposed amends (removing TE Hsu) were not appropriate.

synopsis: The SJC sustained the appeal of the Session of FPC North Port from the Presbytery of Southwest Florida's judgment suspending the Session, reversing the judgment in whole and dismissing all charges. The Commission held that Presbytery's citation and trial of the FPC Session for failure to initiate ecclesiastical process against a church member was constitutionally out of order, compounding the same errors identified in Case 2013-07, as Presbytery had failed to follow the required steps of BCO 40-5 before proceeding against the Session.

title: Complaint of the Session of Hope Community Church vs. Central Carolina Presbytery
topics: session authority over time and place of worship
synopsis: The Session of Hope Community Church complained that Central Carolina Presbytery erred in adopting a policy requiring a multi-site session to eventually particularize its second worship site as a distinct new church. The SJC sustained the complaint, holding that the BCO expressly vests the Session with authority over the time and place of worship and the Presbytery's mandatory particularization requirement exceeded its constitutional powers and was annulled.

The Commission held that once the Original CM Complaint was found out of order, there was no matter for Presbytery to carry forward, and any subsequent instructions to the Session to initiate process were null and void.

title: Marshall v. Pacific Presbytery
synopsis: The SJC found Marshall's complaint judicially out of order on two grounds: that objections raised during a pending judicial case should be reserved for appeal rather than complaint (BCO 32-14; 42-3), and that no complaint is allowable in a judicial case in which an appeal is pending (BCO 43-1). Three commissioners dissented, arguing BCO 32-14 did not apply before trial commenced and that BCO 43-1's exception is limited to pending appeals, not complaints filed before a trial occurs.

title: Gonzales v. Great Lakes Presbytery
topics: lack of acceptance to the Church
habitual failure to perform official functions
synopsis: The SJC sustained TE Gonzales's appeal, reversing and annulling Great Lakes Presbytery's divestiture of him without censure under BCO 34-10, because GLP failed to follow the required procedural steps: it did not record a finding that Gonzales had habitually failed to discharge his official functions, nor determine that his neglect arose only from lack of acceptance to the Church, before proceeding to divestiture. The decision restored Gonzales to good standing in GLP while leaving open GLP's option to pursue proper process under BCO Chapter 31 or 34-10.

canonical_title: Thompson v. South Florida

title: Thompson v. South Florida Presbytery
synopsis: The SJC sustained Thompson's complaint, finding that the Granada Session's judicial commission procedurally erred in imposing excommunication for contumacy before trial because Thompson's behavior did not constitute the immediately censurable kind of contumacy under BCO 32-6.b, and that failing to reach a BCO 38-1 agreement should have led to a trial rather than a pre-trial judgment; the SJC annulled the excommunication and restored the matter to its pre-judgment status as of June 26, 2015.

canonical_title: Thompson v. South Florida

title: Wills v. Metro Atlanta Presbytery
synopsis: The SJC ruled Wills's complaint against MAP's September 15, 2015 action declaring a prior complaint administratively out of order was itself administratively out of order, because BCO 43-2 requires a complaint to first be made to the court whose act is alleged to be in error, and MAP had not yet had the opportunity to respond; Duncan, Evans, and Donahoe dissented.
description: 06/29/15 Metro Atlanta Presbytery (MAP) meets at a called meeting and decides to dissolve Grace North Atlanta (GNA) as an affiliate of MAP and the Presbyterian Church in America. 07/16/15 RE Randy Weekly, RE Steve Flesher, and TE Tolivar Wills file a complaint against the action taken by MAP on 6/29/15 to dissolve GNA.
canonical_title: Wills v. Metro Atlanta

title: Gearhart v. Chicago Metro Presbytery
synopsis: An unsigned complaint was filed on TE Gearhart's behalf by a third party without standing, and TE Gearhart did not contact the PCA Clerk until 77 days after the Presbytery action (47 days past the BCO 43-3 deadline). The SJC ruled the complaint administratively out of order because it was not timely filed and the complainant did not give unqualified ratification.
description: Rationale: Mr. Sanfacon is an inactive ruling elder and member of Tenth Presbyterian Church, Philadelphia. The Record indicates his original Complaint was filed with the Session 18 days after the 60-day window had expired ( _BCO_ 43-2). Thus, it was not timely filed. It was filed on June 16, 2014, against a Session action of March 30, 2014, thus 78 days afterwards. A “second” subsequent Complaint
canonical_title: Gearhart v. Chicago Metro. Administratively out of order

title: Thompson v. South Florida Presbytery
synopsis: John Thompson filed a BCO 43-1 complaint challenging his indictment while his judicial case was still in process before the Session. The SJC ruled the complaint judicially out of order because objections to a trial court's actions must be raised by the defendant during the trial or by appeal after final judgment, not by a separate complaint during the pendency of process.

title: Hardie v. Metro Atlanta Presbytery
synopsis: TE John Hardie appealed his conviction and indefinite suspension by Metro Atlanta Presbytery's judicial commission, raising seventeen specifications of error under six categories including procedural irregularities, improper evidence, refusal of indulgence, prejudice, and disproportionate censure. The SJC denied the appeal, finding no clear error in MAP's proceedings and upholding the indefinite suspension.

title: Bumgarner v. Presbytery of the Mississippi Valley
synopsis: Bumgarner, a ruling elder who had been deposed in 2012, filed a BCO 40-5 application in 2015 seeking reversal of the censure after having previously withdrawn his complaint and acknowledged it could not be resubmitted. The SJC dismissed the application as administratively out of order because it sought to re-file a withdrawn complaint and because BCO 40-3 prohibits reversal of judicial censures except by appeal or complaint.

title: Wills v. Metro Atlanta Presbytery
synopsis: TE Tolivar Wills complained that Metro Atlanta Presbytery improperly dissolved Grace North Atlanta Church without following formal judicial process under BCO 40-6. The SJC denied the complaint, ruling that BCO 13-9(f) grants presbytery inherent authority to dissolve a church without mandating any specific procedure, and that the record did not demonstrate clear error in MAP's exercise of its discretion and judgment.

title: Robertstad v. North Texas Presbytery
synopsis: The SJC found the complaint administratively out of order because it was initially filed via e-mail rather than by hard copy, and was filed by hard copy only after the thirty-day deadline had passed, contrary to BCO 43-3 and OMSJC 18.10.b which prohibit e-mail filing. A concurring opinion provided extensive history of the 2012 and 2014 amendments to BCO 43-3.
description: 04/25/2013 At the Stated Meeting of The Presbytery of the Southwest (PSW), PSW voted to ". . . erect a small committee to shepherd a Teaching Elder (TE) and recommend any actions they deem necessary during his crisis in Roswell." title: Troxell v. Presbytery of the Southwest
synopsis: The SJC partially sustained this complaint, annulling Charge 1 (failure to manage household well relating to a June 2014 divorce) because the 15-month delay before process was instituted in September 2015 violated the BCO 32-20 one-year rule with no evidence the offense had recently become flagrant; other specifications of error were denied, and the improper declaration the TE was 'not in good standing' was sustained but not of a nature to annul other actions.
description: 04/25/2013 At the Stated Meeting of The Presbytery of the Southwest (PSW), PSW voted to ". . . erect a small committee to shepherd a Teaching Elder (TE) and recommend any actions they deem necessary during his crisis in Roswell." title: Doty v. Nashville Presbytery
synopsis: Ms. Doty and 21 others filed charges against a Nashville Presbytery teaching elder first with his congregation's Session (wrong court), then with Nashville Presbytery; Presbytery declined to indict and treated the matter as a report under BCO 31-2 rather than charges. The SJC dismissed the complaint as judicially out of order (11-9-2-2) on the ground that Doty lacked standing under BCO 43-1 as a non-member of Nashville Presbytery; a significant dissent argued that her filing of charges gave her standing under prior precedent (Lyons, Ruff, Fordice).

title: Fordice v. Pacific Northwest Presbytery
synopsis: Fordice complained that Pacific Northwest Presbytery erred by allowing TE Nathan Lewis to confess under BCO 38-1 and closing the matter without adequate process, after its own investigative committee had found a strong presumption of guilt on 18 allegations. The SJC unanimously sustained the complaint and remanded the case to PNW, finding that while use of BCO 38-1 was not itself error, the approved statement of facts was not 'full' as required because it failed to correlate with the underlying allegations.

title: Sartorius v. Siouxlands Presbytery
synopsis: TE Sartorius complained that Siouxlands Presbytery erred in acquitting TE Greg Lawrence on five charges of holding Federal Vision and non-confessional views on baptism, perseverance, and covenant of works at a second trial (2015); the SJC denied the complaint 22-1, holding that the complainant failed to demonstrate clear error in the trial court's factual finding and that BCO 39-3.2 requires great deference to the lower court's verdict. A concurring opinion by RE Donahoe extensively discussed standards of appellate review, defendant testimony supporting the acquittal, and raised concerns about due process rights of acquitted persons.
description: 09/27/02 66 [th] Stated Meeting of the Presbytery of the Siouxlands (“Presbytery”). title: Dailey v. Heritage Presbytery
synopsis: RE Dailey complained that Heritage Presbytery erred in upholding its Moderator's ruling that a church member's document was administratively in order as a complaint under BCO 43-3, after the Session had ruled it out of order. The SJC sustained the complaint, holding that neither triggering condition of BCO 43-3 was met because the Session's ruling that the document did not qualify as a BCO 43-1 complaint was not equivalent to denying it, and the Session had considered and responded to the matter in a timely manner. A substantial dissent argued for a broader interpretation of 'fails to consider' in BCO 43-3.

title: Postles v. Heritage Presbytery
synopsis: RE Postles complained that Heritage Presbytery erred in acting on an ad interim committee recommendation to charge a teaching elder with violating ordination vows 6 and 7 and appointing a prosecutor. The SJC denied the complaint, finding that a committee may recommend judicial action without special authorization (distinguishing from a commission under BCO 15-3), that BCO 31-2 procedures were properly followed, and that the language used by Presbytery in its motion was constitutionally sufficient to institute process.

Case 2017-07 (the companion complaint) was answered by reference to this decision.

title: Lewis v. Presbytery of the Mississippi Valley
synopsis: The SJC ruled that Anna Lewis's BCO 40-5 report filed directly with the SJC was out of order, holding that the SJC's only responsibility under Chapter 40 arises upon referral from the General Assembly per RAO 16-10.c. Six members dissented, arguing the SJC has historically exercised direct jurisdiction over BCO 40-5 reports and that the majority's interpretation was overly narrow and a departure from settled precedent.

synopsis: Arizona Presbytery deposed TE Evans under BCO 38-1 without properly securing his approval of the full statement of facts, as the Investigative Committee's report added additional adverse facts he had not seen or approved. The SJC sustained the complaints of Evans and Pitts et al., annulled the deposition, and remanded the matter to AZP for action consistent with BCO 38-1 requirements; a lengthy concurring opinion by RE Donahoe extensively analyzed BCO 38-1 confession procedures.

title: Crouse v. Northwest Georgia Presbytery
synopsis: The SJC denied a complaint by an ordained Deacon whose nomination for re-election was not advanced by the Session, holding that the Session's exercise of discretion in evaluating a candidate's eligibility for office did not constitute clear error requiring reversal. The dissent argued the proper standard of review was BCO 39-3.4 (de novo for constitutional interpretation), not 39-3.3 (great deference), because the Session applied extra-biblical and extra-constitutional attendance standards in blocking re-election.

synopsis: The SJC sustained a complaint finding that Central Florida Presbytery erred in ruling that Ms. Gendy lacked standing to complain about her removal from church membership under BCO 38-4, holding that a member facing removal retains standing to challenge that removal. The case was remanded to Presbytery to consider the original complaint regarding whether the Session had properly applied BCO 38-3a and 38-4.

The SJC sustained the complaint by a vote of 14-5-2 and remanded the matter to Presbytery to receive a written statement from TE Smith and make a proper determination, with a substantial dissent arguing the Presbytery had in fact complied with the Constitution and that RAO 16-3.e.5 is not part of the BCO.

canonical_title: Crouse v. NW Georgia Presbytery

title: Presbyterian Church in America v. Presbytery of the Mississippi Valley

synopsis: The SJC ruled that the Presbytery of the Mississippi Valley erred in approving a Session's erasure of a member's name from the roll under BCO 38-4 after she pled not guilty to a charge of failing to submit to church government and pursued divorce contrary to the Session's counsel. The SJC found multiple constitutional errors including improper conflation of a not-guilty plea with refusal to submit, failure to consider whether the member would fulfill vows in another branch of the church, failure to notify the member of her erasure, and proceeding to a case without process after dropping formal charges.

title: Sheppard v. Highlands Presbytery
synopsis: TE Craig Sheppard complained that Highlands Presbytery erred by conflating judicial and pastoral procedures after finding a strong presumption of guilt, and by failing to institute process within one year as required by BCO 32-20. The SJC sustained both issues, holding that BCO 31-2 mandates process upon finding a strong presumption of guilt, and that Presbytery was debarred from prosecution because the alleged offense occurred in 2017 and timely process was never instituted; three members dissented on the BCO 32-20 issue, arguing it was not a true statute of limitations and that the matter was never a 'case of scandal.'

title: Harrell et al. v. Covenant Presbytery

parties.complainant_or_appellant: Mr. Paul Harrell et al. (seven members of Christ Redeemer PCA)

synopsis: Seven members of a PCA mission church were tried and censured by a provisional Session for opposing the organizing minister's continuation as permanent pastor. The SJC sustained the appeal and reversed the convictions, holding that the indictments lacked required specificity under BCO 32-5, that the accused had no sinned against the fifth or ninth commandment by expressing opinions about ministerial suitability, and that the right to choose a pastor belongs to the congregation—not the provisional Session. A concurring opinion addressed indictment requirements, standard of review in appeals, and congregational rights in mission church minister selection.
description: 2015 In 2015 Covenant Presbytery established Christ Redeemer, a mission church in Jonesboro, Arkansas and appointed a temporary Session to govern it (“the Session”). title: Turner v. South Florida Presbytery
synopsis: RE Tom Turner complained against South Florida Presbytery's action finding the Session of Cross Community Church guilty of important delinquencies related to alleged sexual abuse incidents involving the pastor's sons, without formal judicial process. The SJC denied the complaint as to Presbytery's right to investigate under BCO 40-4, but sustained it as to the finding of guilt and imposed censures, holding that formal judicial process (indictment, plea, and trial) under BCO 40-6 was required before findings of guilt could be entered, and remanded to Presbytery.

title: Woodham v. South Florida Presbytery
synopsis: TE Michael Woodham complained that South Florida Presbytery erred by excommunicating a Teaching Elder it had previously deposed without first assigning him to membership in a particular church as required by BCO 46-8. synopsis: REs Martinez and Cook complained that Pacific Presbytery erred by tasking its Shepherding Committee to follow up with Valley Presbyterian Church's Session after a dispute about doxologies in worship, alleging this exceeded Presbytery's jurisdictional authority. The SJC denied the complaint on Issue 1 (finding no BCO violation in the visitation itself) but sustained it on Issue 2, concluding Presbytery made clear errors of judgment by sending the Shepherding Committee without sufficiently clear instructions or adequate notice.

title: Psiaki v. Pacific Northwest Presbytery
synopsis: Mr. Psiaki complained that Covenant Presbyterian Church's Session erred in installing officers elected at a congregational meeting that excluded communicant members under age 18 from voting, in violation of the BCO. The SJC sustained the complaint (15-5), holding that all communicant members have a constitutional right to vote in officer elections regardless of age, and that church bylaws may not override BCO provisions; multiple dissenting opinions argued that BCO 20-3 and 24-3's phrase 'good and regular standing' permits local sessions to establish reasonable minimum voting ages.

title: BCO 40-5 Matter re Metropolitan New York Presbytery

synopsis: The SJC, acting on a BCO 40-5 reference from the 50th General Assembly, remitted this matter to Metropolitan New York Presbytery with an injunction to complete its work dealing with TE Higgins and the Session of Trinity Presbyterian Church (Rye, NY) regarding the unconstitutional practice of allowing a woman to expound Scripture in place of a sermon in public worship. The SJC's ruling included a 'Further Note on Procedure' questioning the constitutionality of the Assembly's direct referral to the SJC without first following the multi-step process prescribed by RAO 16-10.c, prompting multiple concurring and dissenting opinions.
description: **PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA** **STANDING JUDICIAL COMMISSION** **CASE No. 2023-13** _**BCO 40-5 MATTER**_ _**re:**_ _**METRO NEW YORK PRESBYTERY**_

title: BCO 40-5 Matter re Northwest Georgia Presbytery
synopsis: The SJC, pursuant to citation by the 50th General Assembly under BCO 40-5, found that Northwest Georgia Presbytery erred egregiously in approving calls and installing three candidates as associate pastors through a constitutionally deficient election process (slate voting, standing vote, failure to record number of voters present). The SJC stayed all further proceedings and declined to annul the installations, finding no PCA precedent for such relief.
description: **PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA** **STANDING JUDICIAL COMMISSION** **Case No. 2023-14** _**BCO 40-5 REPORT**_ _**re:**_ _**NORTHWEST GEORGIA PRESBYTERY**_

title: Huffman v. Tennessee Valley Presbytery
synopsis: TE Huffman filed an appeal after the 50th GA amended BCO 38-1 to change the remedy from complaint to appeal, but his case originated and was nearly complete under the prior rule allowing complaint. The SJC found the case judicially out of order and remanded to Tennessee Valley Presbytery to adjudicate as a complaint under the then-existing rule, resetting the clock for presbytery action.
description: **PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA** **STANDING JUDICIAL COMMISSION** **CASE No. 2023-19** _**TE JARED HUFFMAN**_ _**v.**_ _**TENNESSEE VALLEY PRESBYTERY**_
canonical_title: Appeal of TE Huffman v. TN Valley. Decided 03/08/24. remand 20-1

title: Reynolds v. Pee Dee Presbytery
synopsis: The SJC dismissed Mrs. Reynolds's complaint against Pee Dee Presbytery as untimely, finding it was filed more than sixty days after the Session action in question. The ruling expressly reserved questions about whether she might bring charges as an injured party under BCO 31-5.
description: **CASE No. 2023-02** _**MRS.**_ _**KAPPIE**_ _**REYNOLDS**_ _**V**_ _**.**_ _**PEE**_ _**DEE**_ _**PRESBYTERY**_ **RULING** **ON** **COMPLAINT** October 20, 2023
canonical_title: Mrs. Kappie Reynolds v. Pee Dee. Decided 10/20/23. Out of Order

title: Biese et al. v. Tennessee Valley Presbytery

parties.complainant_or_appellant: TE Ryan Biese, RE Nathan Bowers, RE Wil Davis (et al.)

synopsis: Tennessee Valley Presbytery approved without exception Session minutes planning a youth group Super Bowl Party on Sunday; the SJC denied the complaint because the record was insufficient to find Presbytery erred in its constitutional interpretation. A dissent by RE Eggert argued the complaint was judicially out of order because the SJC lacks constitutional authority to review a presbytery's session-records review decisions under BCO 40-2 and 40-3.
description: 10/14/22 The Tennessee Valley Presbytery (TVP) Committee for the Review of Session Records (RSR) met and reviewed minute submissions from various churches, one set of which was from Redeemer Church in Knoxville, TN. The minutes, dated January 26, 2022, contained this statement: “The youth group will have an outdoor Super Bowl Party on Feb. 7.”
canonical_title: TE Ryan Biese et al. v. TN Valley. Decided 10/20/23. Not Sustained, with concurring and dissenting opinions

title: Evans v. Arizona Presbytery
synopsis: TE John Evans appealed Arizona Presbytery's vote to divest him of his office without censure under BCO 34-10, after he had already affiliated with the Evangelical Presbyterian Church in England and Wales and notified the Presbytery of his withdrawal. The SJC unanimously sustained the appeal and reversed the Presbytery's action, holding that Evans had withdrawn from Presbytery's jurisdiction under BCO 38-3a before the divestiture proceedings began, and instructed the Presbytery to record the irregularity, acknowledge his new membership, and remove his name from the roll.
description: 04/13/20 TE Evans responding to an inquiry from TE Phil Kruis, wrote: “You seem to be asking what expectations I may have of Presbytery in light of the SJC decision [1] ... I believe that Presbytery should note in the minutest ha tia mate in good standing, dissolve any pastoral relationship with the Covenant congregation pending their acting on my resignation ( _BCO_ 23-1) and list my
canonical_title: Appeal of TE Evans v. Arizona. Decided 03/08/24. Sustained, with concurring opinions

title: Myers v. Illiana Presbytery
synopsis: TE Aaron Myers appealed Illiana Presbytery's decision to elevate his censures from indefinite suspension from office and from the Sacraments to excommunication and deposition, without any additional judicial process. The SJC sustained the appeal 21-1, holding that due process principles require some form of process before censure may be elevated, though the majority held that full de novo judicial process was not required; the sole dissent (RE Eggert) argued that Traditional Process under the BCO Rules of Discipline was required, and a concurring opinion (Sartorius) also argued for full BCO process.
description: Issue, Judgment, and Reasoning was written by Greco. The SJC reviewed each part of the proposed decision and approved the final version of the Decision by vote of **21-1**, with 2 absent. 7 RONR (12 th ed.) 35:2 (7). Bankson _Concur_ S. Duncan _Concur_ Maynard _Concur_ Bise _Concur_ Eggert _Dissent_ Neikirk _Concur_

title: Baird et al. v. Grace Presbytery

parties.complainant_or_appellant: TE Knox Baird et al. (Session members of First Presbyterian Church, Hattiesburg, MS)

synopsis: The SJC sustained multiple complaints brought by elders of First Presbyterian Church Hattiesburg against Grace Presbytery's Commission and Committee, finding: the Blue Hen complaint was moot and never properly filed; the Commission improperly directed the Clerk to produce personal communications beyond official records; Presbytery improperly directed the Session to inform the congregation; and the Commission's stay of pending trials was unconstitutional because BCO 40-5 requires citation of the lower court before any stay can be imposed. Issue 5 (appointment of Committee for BCO 40-5 Reports) was denied.
description: 05/18/22 by a vote of 17-0 the Session voted to engage a third-party mediator to assist in identifying and resolving conflicts within the church. Session minutes indicate that this followed a period of “many trials and difficulties,” and note “disagreement between members of the staff.” 05/25/22 The Session voted 13-4 to engage the Blue Hen firm as a mediator.
canonical_title: & 2023-08 TE Knox Baird et al. v. Grace. Decided 03/08/24. Sustained, with concurring opinions

title: Turner v. South Florida Presbytery
topics: access to courts of the church
synopsis: The SJC ruled RE Tom Turner's complaints administratively out of order for lack of standing, because Cross Community Church had voted to disaffiliate from the PCA on February 12, 2023, rendering Turner no longer a PCA member. Two members dissented, arguing CCC should have been afforded hearing and formal process before being denied access to PCA courts.
description: **CASE Nos. 2023-15 & 2023-17** _**MR. TOM TURNER**_ _**v.**_ _**SOUTH FLORIDA PRESBYTERY**_ **RULING ON COMPLAINTS** March 7, 2024

title: Flatgard v. Metro Atlanta Presbytery
synopsis: TE Flatgard was deposed by Metro Atlanta Presbytery on three charges related to conduct during his marital separation and divorce; the SJC sustained his appeal 14-3, finding the Record insufficient to support a guilty verdict and censure on the charges of failing to maintain elder qualifications, failing to love his wife, and failing to uphold ordination vows. The case generated extensive concurring and dissenting opinions debating appellate deference standards under BCO 39-3.2, 39-3.3, and 39-3.4.
description: **CASE 2023-12** **TE ANDREW FLATGARD** **v.** **METRO ATLANTA PRESBYTERY** **DECISION ON APPEAL** May 24, 2024

title: Bulkeley & Gillikin v. Highlands Presbytery

parties.complainant_or_appellant: TE Craig Bulkeley and TE Skip Gillikin

synopsis: TEs Bulkeley and Gillikin complained that Highlands Presbytery refused to record their dissent against Presbytery's act of recording a letter from the Arden Presbyterian Session affirming the Martins' Christian character. The SJC denied the complaint but went further, annulling Presbytery's recording of the Session letter as contrary to BCO 14-7 and the finality of the prior Sheppard decision (Case 2022-04), generating multiple concurring and dissenting opinions about the SJC's authority to reach issues not raised by the parties.

title: Sears v. Nashville Presbytery
synopsis: TE Ian Sears appealed his deposition by Nashville Presbytery, which handled the case as a BCO 38-1 case without process after he signed a written confession of guilt for two incidents of sexual misconduct in 2010 and 2011. The SJC unanimously denied the appeal on all five specifications of error, affirming the Presbytery's judgment and the validity of the BCO 38-1 process, including its procedures for drafting the confession and refusing to allow Sears to withdraw it after censure was imposed.

title: Martinez v. Pacific Presbytery
synopsis: RE Martinez complained that Pacific Presbytery erred by sustaining a complaint against the Valley Presbyterian Church Session's decision to close worship with benedictions rather than doxologies. The SJC sustained the complaint 19-1, holding that the Session had constitutional authority under BCO 12-5.e to make this liturgical decision and that it was not a clear error of judgment or discretion.
description: 07/11/22 Stated meeting of the Session of Valley Presbyterian Church (hereafter "VPC") at which by vote of 5-3, the Session, approved a motion "to have God's blessings as formal benedictions to conclude the service and not doxologies." 08/09/22 Session members RE Riedinger and Shaw and filed a timely _BCO_ 43-1 Complaint with the Session regarding that decision. [.15]

title: Kang et al. v. Korean Central Presbytery
synopsis: Three Teaching Elders complained that Korean Central Presbytery clearly erred in declining to indict a Teaching Elder after a BCO 31-2 investigation into allegations of financial impropriety, spiritual abuse, and physical abuse. The SJC held that KCP did not clearly err, given the age of the allegations and the deference owed to lower courts on factual and discretionary matters; the dissent argued that KCP's investigation was constitutionally deficient for failing to exercise due diligence.

title: Suh v. Korean Southeastern Presbytery
synopsis: TE Sam Jung Suh appealed his indefinite suspension by Korean Southeastern Presbytery following a trial on charges of financial misconduct. The SJC reversed the conviction in whole, finding that the pre-investigative and BCO 31-2 investigation phases were marred by grave constitutional errors including failure to comply with BCO 27-5, failure to demand satisfactory explanations from TE Suh, improper expansion of the investigation, and absence of a valid antecedent condition necessary to institute process; multiple dissents argued the SJC should have deferred to the lower court's factual findings and trial verdict.
description: 09/26/22 Three Teaching Elders signed a “Formal Petition” to Korean Southeastern Presbytery (“KSEP”) alleging a “formal charge of financial misconduct” against TE Suh, the pastor of Korean First Presbyterian Church (“KFPC”). The document claimed that TE Suh had “publicly admitted his wife deposited church funds from the sale of church property into her and his

title: Complaint of Mueller v. South Florida Presbytery
synopsis: The complaint was withdrawn by agreement of the complainant, the Presbytery Clerk, and the Chairman of Presbytery's Judicial Commission on December 21, 2023. The SJC approved the request and remanded the matter to Presbytery without instructions and without prejudice.

Equine couples therapy was begun. 02/16/23 The Session of WHPC met with TE and Mrs. Moehn to discuss the state of their marriage and correspondence each

title: Hyland v. Chicago Metro Presbytery
synopsis: A terminated church employee filed a complaint against Chicago Metro Presbytery after Presbytery declined to indict the pastors she accused, but Presbytery ruled her complaint out of order for lack of standing under BCO 43-1. The SJC, voting 12-12, left the Presbytery's decision standing, finding the complaint judicially out of order because the complainant lacked standing to file a BCO 43-1 complaint against Presbytery.
description: 03/19/21 Emily Hyland, a member of Naperville Presbyterian Church (NPC) and an employee of the church since June 1, 2013, was terminated from her employment. At the time of her termination, she had been serving NPC in the position of Director of Operations. 04/09/21 NPC Session received notification of representation from a law
canonical_title: Hyland v. Chicago Metro Presbytery. [ M50GA, p. 776]

title: Moehn v. Westminster Presbytery
synopsis: TE Andy Moehn complained that Westminster Presbytery erred in its BCO 31-2 investigation of his wife Kacey Moehn regarding alleged desertion and in declaring the question of biblical divorce grounds moot. The SJC sustained the complaint 21-1-0, finding that Presbytery used an extra-confessional interpretation of WCF 24.6 requiring excommunication before desertion grounds could apply, dismissed relevant evidence, and improperly adjudicated TE Moehn's role in the marriage breakdown without due process; the SJC annulled Presbytery's Recommendations 1 and 3 and found a new investigation was not in order since Mrs. Moehn had left PCA jurisdiction.

title: Gozé v. Hills and Plains Presbytery
synopsis: The SJC dismissed the appeal as judicially out of order because it was not timely filed within 30 days of notification of the Session's decision per BCO 42-4. RE Jim Eggert filed a dissent arguing that the 30-day period never began because the Session never reduced its decision to writing and delivered it in a prescribed manner, so the appeal should have been deemed timely.
description: **CASE 2024-10** **MR. PAUL GOZÉ** **v.** **HILLS AND PLAINS PRESBYTERY** **DECISION ON APPEAL** **February 19, 2025**

title: Colquitt v. Houston Metro Presbytery
synopsis: TE Colquitt complained that Houston Metro Presbytery's Judicial Commission erred in ruling the Gleason Complaint out of order. The SJC answered Colquitt's Complaint by reference to its ruling in Case 2024-02 (Gleason v. Houston Metro Presbytery), finding that the BCO 43 complaint process is not a proper avenue for accusations, and denied the complaint 21-0-0.
description: **CASE 2024-13** **TE RICHARD COLQUITT** **v.** **HOUSTON METRO PRESBYTERY** **DECISION ON COMPLAINT** **October 17, 2024**

title: Mueller v. South Florida Presbytery
synopsis: RE Mueller complained that the Redlands Community Church Session erred in allowing Alcoholics Anonymous to use church facilities for weekly meetings, ultimately reaching the SJC after the Session's 14-month failure to consider the complaint and South Florida Presbytery's denial. The SJC denied the complaint, finding AA's use of church property was a matter of Session discretion under BCO 12-5 that did not rise to clear constitutional error, while expressing concern about the Session's lengthy failure to act on the original complaint.
description: **CASE 2024-16** **RE WILLIAM MUELLER** **v.** **SOUTH FLORIDA PRESBYTERY** **DECISION ON COMPLAINT** **February 19, 2025**

title: Harris v. Wisconsin Presbytery
synopsis: RE Harris, as a Presbytery prosecutor, complained that Wisconsin Presbytery's Judicial Commission erred in dismissing both indictments against a pastor for alleged pastoral care failures. The SJC denied the complaint 18-4-0, finding the JC's dismissal of Indictment 1 under BCO 32-20 (passage of time) was within its discretion despite noting some troubling reasoning by JC members, and that the dismissal of Indictment 2 was authorized because the charge arose from a private offense never brought through proper process; four members dissented as to Indictment 1, arguing the JC clearly erred by conducting a de facto trial with only the accused's unsworn testimony.

title: Gleason v. Houston Metro Presbytery
synopsis: Mr. Gleason filed a BCO 43 complaint alleging the Session sinned by approving a Personnel Committee Statement read to the congregation regarding an associate pastor's call dissolution. The SJC ruled the complaint judicially out of order and unable to be put in order because BCO 43 complaints are not a constitutionally permissible avenue for processing accusations of individual sin or requiring repentance from a church court, as complaints address errors of courts rather than sins of individuals.
description: **CASE 2024-02** **MR. GREG GLEASON** **v.** **HOUSTON METRO PRESBYTERY** **DECISION ON COMPLAINT** **October 17, 2024**

title: DeWitt v. Nashville Presbytery
synopsis: TE DeWitt requested withdrawal of his appeal against Nashville Presbytery on November 21, 2024. The SJC dismissed the case per the Appellant's request.
description: **CASE 2024-21** **TE CHARLES DEWITT** **v.** **NASHVILLE PRESBYTERY** **RULING ON APPEAL** **February 19, 2025**

title: Benyola v. Central Florida Presbytery
synopsis: Mr. Benyola filed a complaint against Central Florida Presbytery but lacked standing as a non-member of any PCA congregation (BCO 43-1). The SJC dismissed the complaint as administratively out of order, controlled by its prior decisions in Cases 2022-17, 18, and 19 involving the same complainant, voted 21-0-0 with 2 absent and 1 disqualified.
description: **CASE 2024-21** **TE CHARLES DEWITT** **v.** **NASHVILLE PRESBYTERY** **RULING ON APPEAL** **February 19, 2025**

title: Gozé v. Hills and Plains Presbytery
synopsis: The SJC reviewed the complaint and found it administratively out of order and unable to be put in order because it was not a complaint against an act of a court (the Session).
description: **CASE 2024-04** **PAUL GOZÉ** **v.** **HILLS AND PLAINS PRESBYTERY** **DECISION ON COMPLAINT** **October 17, 2024**

title: Bigelow v. North Florida Presbytery
synopsis: TE Kevin Bigelow complained against multiple actions of North Florida Presbytery related to the dissolution of his call as Assistant Pastor of Christ Church East (where he was also serving as planting pastor at Christ Church Beaches). The SJC sustained the complaint in part (22-0-0), finding that Presbytery erred in ruling four of Bigelow's five complaints out of order; however, after reviewing the substance of all five complaints, the SJC denied each on the merits, concluding Presbytery committed no clear error in dissolving the call, that no congregational vote of CCB was constitutionally required, and that no formal discipline without process had occurred.
description: **CASE 2024-05** **TE KEVIN BIGELOW** **v.** **NORTH FLORIDA PRESBYTERY** **DECISION ON COMPLAINT** **March 6, 2025**

The SJC unanimously concluded SCP had fully complied with the August 12, 2024 Citation, while noting it took no position on the contents of the responses.

synopsis: The SJC ruled the complaint out of order because the complainant, Ms. Jennifer Bigelow, was not a member of the Presbytery against whose action she complained, and therefore lacked standing to file a BCO 43-1 complaint. The decision was unanimous at 23-0-0 with 1 absent.

synopsis: TE Jeffrey Black's complaint against Eastern Carolina Presbytery's July 1999 ruling that a transferring licentiate's view on the length of the creation days did not constitute an exception to the Westminster Standards. canonical_title: Morse v. Covenant Presbytery
title: Morse v. Covenant Presbytery
synopsis: The Standing Judicial Commission addressed three issues regarding actions of Covenant Presbytery involving TE Daniel R. Morse and Grace Presbyterian Church. The Commission ruled that teaching elders Morse and Crenshaw, having renounced communion with the PCA by joining the Reformed Episcopal Church, should be removed from presbytery rolls under BCO 38-3. The Commission found no provision requiring churches to notify presbytery before withdrawing from the PCA. Finally, the Commission determined that receiving and recording a protest is an administrative act, not a judicial decision, and that complaints regarding such actions are barred by BCO 45-5.

canonical_title: Sartorius v. Siouxlands — M43GA, 2015 Chattanooga, p. 528. Sustained in part, 18-1. C-Op 9. C-Op 3. C-Op 2. Summary: Alleged P procedurally erred in a doctrinal trial of TE Lawrence. Also alleged error in the not guilty verdict. SJC ruled error in procedures and instructed new trial. Issue: Did the
title: Complaint: TE Art Sartorius vs. Siouxlands Presbytery
topics: rules of evidence in ecclesiastical courts
synopsis: The Standing Judicial Commission sustained the complaint of TE Art Sartorius and six others against Presbytery of Siouxlands, finding that the Presbytery erred when it approved its Judicial Commission's verdict acquitting TE Gregory Lawrence of five charges related to Federal Vision theology and unconventional views on baptism and apostasy. The SJC identified serious procedural errors, specifically that the Judicial Commission admitted exculpatory post-indictment written statements from the defendant without subjecting them to cross-examination, contrary to BCO 35-5. The case was remanded to Presbytery for a new trial with detailed instructions regarding evidence procedures and Presbytery oversight.

title: Appeal of TE Sung K. Kim vs. Korean Capital Presbytery
synopsis: Case 2002-17 (Appeal of TE Sung K. Kim vs. Korean Capital Presbytery) was declared judicially out of order because the appellant did not submit to a regular trial as required by BCO 42-2. The appellant was found guilty of contumacy and deposed from office by the Presbytery before the appeal was filed.

title: Appeal of Paul W. Lee v. Korean Southwest — M33GA, 2005 Chattanooga, p. 107. Sustained 19-0 Summary: Alleged P erred in process and judgment in a TE's trial on three charges. synopsis: Appeal by TE Paul W. Lee against Korean Southwest Presbytery's judgment finding him guilty of disobeying citations, rejecting admonitions, and other charges. The Standing Judicial Commission found KSWP erred on all issues through procedural violations including improper admonishment of judicial commission members mid-trial, failure to record trial proceedings, denial of cross-examination rights, and improper censure. Case remanded for retrial or dismissal.

canonical_title: Thornton v. Westminster
title: Thornton v. Westminster
synopsis: Westminster Presbytery was found to have erred in its BCO 31-2 investigation of secret taping by TE Frank Smith by failing to obtain full witness testimony and investigate legal ramifications; case remanded. The court found no proof of partiality and declined to require establishment of unbiblical criteria for secret taping. Vote: 18 concurring, 1 dissenting (RE Frederick Neikirk), 2 recused, 1 abstained, 2 absent.

title: Complaint of DE Joong Min Soh vs. Philadelphia Presbytery
synopsis: Case 2008-08 (DE Joong Min Soh v. Philadelphia Presbytery) appears in the SJC docket list as found to be out of order. No substantive decision or opinion was issued.

canonical_title: Nichols & Couch v. James River
title: Nichols & Couch v. James River
synopsis: Complaint of RE Leland Nichols and RE Sam Couch vs. James River Presbytery concerning West End Presbyterian Church's attempts to dissolve its pastoral relationship with TE Robert Wilson. Combined with cases 2001-34 and 2002-03. SJC sustained the complaints (13-3), holding that JRP had no constitutional basis to delay granting the congregation's properly voted request to dissolve the pastoral relationship, though several of the 17 individual complaints were ruled judicially out of order.

canonical_title: McNeil v. Chesapeake Presbytery
title: Mr. Michael A. McNeil vs. Chesapeake Presbytery
synopsis: Case docketed for the 2011 General Assembly (39th GA). The case was found to be Judicially Out of Order and was not decided on the merits. This is a continuation of Michael McNeil's numerous complaints against Chesapeake Presbytery, appearing alongside similar cases 2010-09 through 2010-14, all of which were similarly ruled Judicially Out of Order.

canonical_title: Staley v. North Texas
title: Staley v. North Texas
bco_cited_as: Implied: Standing requirement for PCA church members
bco_cited_current: Implied: Standing requirement for PCA church members
bco_chapters_ascited: Implied: Standing requirement for PCA church members
bco_chapters_current: Implied: Standing requirement for PCA church members
synopsis: The SJC found the complaint filed by Thomas Clark Staley against North Texas Presbytery to be out of order on the grounds that Mr. Staley had no standing as he was not a member of a PCA church. The decision turned on the jurisdictional requirement that only PCA church members may bring complaints before the Standing Judicial Commission.

canonical_title: Session of Living Word v. Pacific Northwest
title: Complaint of Session of Living Word PC vs. Pacific NW Presbytery
synopsis: Case listed in the SJC docket as 'Complaint of Session of Living Word PC vs. Pacific NW Presbytery' but was found out of order and not heard on the merits. Living Word Church withdrew from the denomination on 11/16/05.

canonical_title: Reference from Central Carolina Presbytery
title: Reference from Central Carolina Presbytery
synopsis: The Standing Judicial Commission declined to adjudicate a reference from Central Carolina Presbytery regarding complaints by William A. Conrad against the Interim Session of Prosperity Presbyterian Church. The panel found that additional related complaints had been lodged that were not formally referred to the Commission, and adjudicating the referred matters would render these additional complaints moot without giving them proper consideration under BCO 41-5. The case was referred back to the Central Carolina Presbytery.

canonical_title: Carpenter v. Siouxlands
title: Carpenter v. Siouxlands
synopsis: The SJC found that Presbytery of Siouxlands erred in denying a complaint that sought the appointment of a committee to conduct a BCO 31-2 investigation into whether TE Greg Lawrence held or taught views associated with Federal Vision theology contrary to the PCA's doctrinal standards. The Court directed that the presbytery conduct the required investigation and established that credible reports affecting Christian character warrant investigation regardless of the evidentiary standards required for conviction.

canonical_title: Peter B. Kim v. Korean Eastern
title: Complaint of TE Peter B. Kim vs. Korean Eastern Presbytery
synopsis: Case 2005-12 was a complaint filed by TE Peter B. Kim against Korean Eastern Presbytery. The case was found to be out of order and was not heard on its merits by the Standing Judicial Commission.

canonical_title: TE John Kim vs. Texas Presbytery [ M5GA (1977): 89-90. Case was deemed abandoned
title: TE John Kim vs. Texas Presbytery [ M5GA (1977): 89-90. Case was deemed abandoned
synopsis: John Kim appealed a decision of Texas Presbytery to the Judicial Commission. The Commission found Mr. Kim's explanation for his absence not satisfactory under BCO 43-10, and the case was deemed abandoned. The Commission voted 12-4 that the absence explanation was not satisfactory.

synopsis: Communication from Elder Chung In Mo et al. requesting general review and control against action by Korean Presbyterian Church of Washington, D.C. and the Korean Southeastern Presbytery, alleging improper and unjust abuses of the discipline process and untimely filing refusal. The 15th GA appointed a special committee to inspect all written records and investigate, to report back to the 16th General Assembly. No decision rendered at 15th GA.

title: Complaint of Russell Warren vs. Chesapeake Presbytery
synopsis: Case 2009-32 was a complaint filed by Russell Warren against Chesapeake Presbytery. The Standing Judicial Commission found the case to be administratively out of order and therefore did not issue a substantive decision on the merits.

canonical_title: Appeal of Samuel Hong v. Korean Capital — M30GA, 2002 Birmingham, p. 142. Sustained 16-1 Summary: Alleged P commission did not have jurisdiction to conduct trial of a Deacon. Issues: Was P and/or its Commission appointed on January 8, 2001 the proper court to assume original jurisdiction to receive
title: Appeal Deacon Samuel Hong vs. Korean Capital Presbytery
synopsis: The Standing Judicial Commission reversed the Korean Capital Presbytery's censure of Deacon Samuel Hong, finding that the Presbytery lacked original jurisdiction over communicant members and should have been tried in the Session. The court held that regardless of the merits of the underlying charges (contumacy and making false statements), the procedural violation of jurisdiction was fatal to the action.

canonical_title: Williams v. Eastern Carolina
title: Complaint RE Keith A. Williams vs. Eastern Carolina Presbytery
synopsis: Complaint of RE Keith A. Williams vs. Eastern Carolina Presbytery, docketed in 2001 but withdrawn prior to Standing Judicial Commission hearing. Case was listed in the docket at the 30th General Assembly (2002) but withdrew before decision could be rendered.

canonical_title: Preg et al. v. Missouri Presbytery
title: Preg et al. v. Missouri Presbytery
synopsis: Complaint by Westminster Reformed Presbyterian Church session against Missouri Presbytery for establishing a standing committee to conduct required annual visitations to member churches. Commission sustained the complaint, finding that presbytery exceeded its constitutional authority under BCO 13-9 to require visits without expressed provision or grounds in the BCO.

canonical_title: TE H.L. Webb and TE T. Miller vs. Pacific Presbytery [ M3GA (1975): 48-50]. Dismissed
title: TE H.L. Webb and TE T. Miller vs. Pacific Presbytery [ M3GA (1975): 48-50]. Dismissed
synopsis: Judicial complaint by Rev. Harold L. Webb and Rev. Thomas Miller against Pacific Presbytery regarding its August 4, 1975 meeting conducted without the constitutionally required quorum of three teaching and three ruling elders. The Committee on Judicial Business determined the actions were irregular but recommended dismissal after Pacific Presbytery met in duly constituted session on September 10, 1975, with proper quorum and ratified all actions from the August 4 meeting.

canonical_title: Appeal of Nickoley v. Rocky Mountain [ M26GA (1998): 122]. Sustained 17-0 — Summary: TE alleged he was convicted and deposed w/o proper process. Issue : Did Rocky Mountain Presbytery err in receiving and immediately acting upon a motion to cite Rev. Bruce Nickoley for contumacy and rendering the jud
title: Appeal of Nickoley v. Rocky Mountain [ M26GA (1998): 122]. Sustained 17-0 — Summary: TE alleged he was convicted and deposed w/o proper process. Issue : Did Rocky Mountain Presbytery err in receiving and immediately acting upon a motion to cite Rev. Bruce Nickoley for contumacy and rendering the jud
synopsis: Rocky Mountain Presbytery erred in immediately deposing Rev. Bruce Nickoley for contumacy without giving him reasonable opportunity to defend himself under the due process provisions of the Book of Church Order. The case was remanded to the presbytery with recommendations to either institute a formal prosecution with due process or revoke the deposition and reinstate Nickoley.

canonical_title: Woodward v. Western Carolina Presbytery
title: Woodward v. Western Carolina Presbytery
synopsis: Robert Woodward, along with James Linton and Kirk Lyons, filed complaints against Western Carolina Presbytery alleging it erred when it did not find a strong presumption of guilt against TE Craig Bulkeley and when it failed to properly investigate grievances due to procedural failures and alleged denial of due process to the accusers. The cases were combined with Case 2009-5 (TE Martin Payne complaint) due to similar circumstances.

Issue: Did North Georgia P
title: Complaint of Devin & Tracey Ehrlich vs. North Georgia Presbytery
topics: peace and purity of the church
synopsis: Complaint of Devin and Tracey Ehrlich against North Georgia Presbytery regarding the Presbytery's denial of the Ehrlichs' complaint about the Intown Community Church Session's instruction prohibiting distribution of an open letter criticizing a pastor under investigation. The SJC held that the Session acted within its authority in preventing distribution of the letter, as it would disturb the peace of the church after the Presbytery had investigated and dismissed the underlying allegations against the pastor.

canonical_title: Hunt v. Western Carolina
title: Complaint of Mr. ME Hunt vs. Western Carolina Presbytery
synopsis: Case of complaint by ME Hunt vs. Western Carolina Presbytery. The case appeared on the SJC docket for 2004 but was found out of order and therefore not decided on the merits.

canonical_title: Conrad et al. v. Central Carolina Presbytery. (Case #1)
title: Conrad et al. v. Central Carolina Presbytery. (Case #1)
synopsis: Central Carolina Presbytery remanded complaints to an Interim Session Commission rather than adjudicating them directly. The SJC confirmed this action, finding that the Commission appointed as Interim Session was the proper court of original jurisdiction and remanding the complaints for adjudication by that Commission.

canonical_title: Appeal of Sang Roo Ryoo v. Korean Capital — M30GA, 2002 Birmingham, p. 151. Sustained 17-0 Summary: Alleged P commission did not follow BCO procedures in judicial process of a TE. Remanded for new trial in compliance with BCO 32 and 34. Issues: Did the Judicial Commission follow BCO procedures in in
title: Appeal TE Sang Soo Ryoo vs. Korean Capital Presbytery
synopsis: The Standing Judicial Commission found that the Judicial Commission of Korean Capital Presbytery did not follow proper procedures under BCO 32 and 34 in adjudicating charges against appellant TE Sang Soo Ryoo. Specifically, no indictment was ever properly served on the accused, and there was no clear record of proceedings. The case was remanded to Presbytery for a new trial with full compliance to BCO 32 and 34 procedures.

canonical_title: Roberts v. New River Presbytery
title: Roberts v. New River Presbytery
synopsis: Virgil B. Roberts, a ruling elder, complained that New River Presbytery erred by not accepting the resignation of Rev. Don Post, Jr. as pastor of Providence Reformed Presbyterian Church, when the minister had tendered his resignation, the congregation had voted to accept it (28-16), and the commissioners appeared before Presbytery. The SJC sustained the complaint, holding that BCO 23-1 requires the Presbytery to accept and dissolve the pastoral relationship when these conditions are met. The Court held that the discretion given to a Presbytery under BCO 23-1 does not give it power to prevent the implementation of a valid decision of a congregation. Vote: 16-3 with dissent.

canonical_title: Jackson v. Northwest Georgia
title: Complaint: RE Warren Jackson vs. Northwest Georgia Presbytery
synopsis: Case 2013-02 (RE Warren Jackson vs. Northwest Georgia Presbytery) was found to be Administratively Out of Order by the Standing Judicial Commission. No substantive decision on the merits was reached.

title: In re Korean Eastern Presbytery ........................... Completed, 588

title: Stuart Merriam vs. Tennessee Valley Presbytery (Appeal) ... p. 76
synopsis: TE Stuart Merriam, a minister transferred from PCUSA to Tennessee Valley Presbytery in 1986, was subject to ecclesiastical discipline based on his criminal conviction in Papua New Guinea. The SJC sustained Merriam's appeal, finding that TVP violated due process principles and procedures under the BCO, particularly by failing to appoint counsel and conducting an inadequate trial. The case was remanded to TVP for a new trial following proper BCO procedures.

canonical_title: Wichter Memorial Re: Case 2004-05 Blevins v. Westminster

canonical_title: Nickoley v. Rocky Mountain [ M26GA (1998): 129}. Administratively out of order because complaint involved a matter on which an appeal was pending
title: Nickoley v. Rocky Mountain [ M26GA (1998): 129}. Administratively out of order because complaint involved a matter on which an appeal was pending
synopsis: The SJC found that Rocky Mountain Presbytery erred in immediately deposing Rev. Bruce Nickoley from ministry on a contumacy charge without providing him due process rights and a full trial. The case was remanded for either proper trial or revocation of the deposition judgment.

canonical_title: Bogue et al. v. Ascension Presbytery
title: Bogue et al. v. Ascension Presbytery
synopsis: Carl Bogue et al. v. Presbytery of the Ascension: A multi-year judicial dispute concerning the ordination of TE Kurt Lutjens of Ascension Presbytery. The complainants challenged Presbytery's acceptance of Lutjens' modified positions on Lord's Supper invitation requirements, baptism, and women deacons. The commission denied all major specifications, finding that Lutjens' views had substantially changed to conform with church standards, and that the Presbytery's response to a General Assembly exception was properly grounded in fact and pastoral concern, though procedurally imperfect in attempting to clarify prior minutes.

canonical_title: Appeal of Fitzsimmons v. Evangel
title: Appeal of Fitzsimmons v. Evangel
synopsis: The Standing Judicial Commission found that Evangel Presbytery erred in failing to notify appellant E.L. Fitzsimmons of the time and place of the hearing on his appeal and in failing to adjudicate the appeal once it was found to be in order. The case was remanded to Evangel Presbytery for proper rehearing and adjudication in accordance with BCO 42-8.

Commission recommended that the assembly's action in finding the complaint in order and electing the commission be reconsidered. Assembly adopted the recommendation, rescinded the original determination, and found the complaint not in order, thus disestablishing the commission.

Complaint filed against that action, and it was sustained. The TE was reexamined, but he did not pass. This new Complaint alleged the reexamination was unconstitu
title: Complaint TE Larry Edison, et. al. vs. Southwest Florida
synopsis: Case involved Southwest Florida Presbytery's handling of theological examination and ministerial call for TE Bryan Gregory. SJC partially sustained the complaint, ruling that presbytery had right to hear and sustain complaint about theological examination but erred in its subsequent actions to rescind membership and reexamine the minister without due process, as once membership was conferred it could only be removed through disciplinary proceedings.

Issues: Was P and/or its Commission appointed on January 8, 2001 the proper court to assume original jurisdiction to receive and
title: Appeal RE Joo Bok Suh vs. Korean Capital Presbytery
synopsis: Appeal by RE Joo Bok Suh challenging the Korean Capital Presbytery's attempt to exercise original jurisdiction over charges of making false statements on official documents related to church trustee replacement. The Standing Judicial Commission reversed the presbytery's action, holding that original jurisdiction over communicant members must reside with the Session of the church to which they belong, not with the Presbytery.

canonical_title: Wright v. Eastern Carolina — M32GA, 2004 Pittsburgh, p. 106. JOO 17-1. D-Op Summary: Session sent BCO 41 Reference on theological question. C was against P's answer. SJC ruled answer was only advice and thus not a complainable P action.
title: Wright v. Eastern Carolina — M32GA, 2004 Pittsburgh, p. 106. JOO 17-1. D-Op Summary: Session sent BCO 41 Reference on theological question. C was against P's answer. SJC ruled answer was only advice and thus not a complainable P action.
synopsis: RE Paul M. Wright filed a complaint against Eastern Carolina Presbytery regarding its response to a Reference from White Oak Presbyterian Church Session on a doctrinal issue. The SJC found the case judicially out of order because the Presbytery's action was advice only in response to a Reference for advice under BCO 41-1, and such advisory actions are not complainable under BCO 43. The Panel recommended the case be found out of order. Vote: 17 concurring, 1 dissenting (RE J. Howard Donahoe), 1 abstaining, 4 absent.

canonical_title: Appeal of Rountree v. Covenant — M29GA, 2001 Dallas, p. 82. Not Sustained 15-0 Summary: Alleged P erred by divesting TE w/o censure after 7 years w/o call. Issue: Did P err in its divestiture without censure of TE Lawrence Rountree? Judgment: No. P acted within its constitutional prerogatives in div
title: Appeal of Rountree v. Covenant — M29GA, 2001 Dallas, p. 82. Not Sustained 15-0 Summary: Alleged P erred by divesting TE w/o censure after 7 years w/o call. Issue: Did P err in its divestiture without censure of TE Lawrence Rountree? Judgment: No. P acted within its constitutional prerogatives in div
synopsis: Covenant Presbytery properly divested Teaching Elder Lawrence Rountree without censure after he remained on presbytery rolls for over seven years without a valid call to ministry, residing outside presbytery bounds, with no prospects for ministry. The Standing Judicial Commission sustained presbytery's divestiture action as constitutional and appropriate.
