canonical_title: Oxbalt et al. v. Pacific Presbytery
title: Ozbalt et al v. Pacific Presbytery ..........................Completed, 687
synopsis: RE Sean Ozbalt and RE Erin Barr filed complaints challenging Pacific Presbytery's handling of TE Rankin Wilbourne's case. The SJC sustained the complaints, finding that the reading of a judicial commission report not agreed upon by the accused violated BCO 38-1, that the sins were not base and flagitious, and that procedural constitutional violations occurred. The SJC annulled the censure of deposition and recommended treating the 17-month censure as a definite suspension from office.

canonical_title: Sung Keon Kim v. Korean Capital — M30GA, 2002 Birmingham, p. 109. Sustained 17-3. Summary: Alleged P erred by "replacing" Session with a P commission w/o congregational approval. (Two groups claimed to be rightful Session.) Issue: Did P err in its action of October 9, 2000 "... to suspend the functi
title: Sung Keon Kim v. Korean Capital — M30GA, 2002 Birmingham, p. 109. Sustained 17-3. Summary: Alleged P erred by "replacing" Session with a P commission w/o congregational approval. (Two groups claimed to be rightful Session.) Issue: Did P err in its action of October 9, 2000 "... to suspend the functi
synopsis: The Standing Judicial Commission sustained the complaint of TE Sung Keon Kim against Korean Capital Presbytery's action to suspend the Session of Korean Presbyterian Church Washington (KPCW) without congregational consent. The SJC held that the Presbytery's appointment of an administrative and judicial commission to replace the Session violated Preliminary Principle Six and BCO 16-2, which protect the congregation's inalienable right to elect those in authority. The SJC annulled the subsequent actions of the commission.

canonical_title: Request for Reference from Blue Ridge Presbytery
title: Request for Reference from Blue Ridge Presbytery
synopsis: Blue Ridge Presbytery requested that the Standing Judicial Commission hear and decide a complaint because of claimed bias and constitutional questions. The SJC denied the request for reference, holding that the record did not demonstrate affirmative bias, serious division, constitutional questions, or delicate issues that would warrant reference under BCO 41-2. The presbytery was directed to hear and decide the complaint itself.

canonical_title: Appeal of Tarter v. Evangel — M42GA, 2014 Houston, p. 539. Sustained 15-0. Summary: TE was laboring out of bounds in Ireland. canonical_title: Eliot Lee v. Korean Eastern
title: Eliot Lee v. Korean Eastern
synopsis: The Standing Judicial Commission sustained the complaints of TE Eliot Lee against Korean Eastern Presbytery (cases 2007-09 and 2007-10). KEP erred when it empowered the Pulpit Commission to act on behalf of the church session without proper consent, and erred when it filed a civil action against TE Lee to adjudicate ecclesiastical matters. The decision affirms the non-hierarchical, voluntary nature of the PCA and its limited jurisdiction in civil matters.

Did the Presbytery of North Florida err constitutionally or procedurally whe
title: Complaint TE Anthony Dallison vs. Northern Florida Presbytery
synopsis: TE Anthony Dallison appealed his deposition from the office of Teaching Elder by North Florida Presbytery, arguing that presbytery lacked authority to increase his censure from indefinite suspension to deposition without conducting a new trial. The SJC affirmed presbytery's decision, holding that where a teaching elder has confessed guilt under BCO 38-1, presbytery may impose additional censure based on evidence of impenitence without initiating new charges, and such disciplinary discretion is within presbytery's constitutional authority.

canonical_title: Luther L. Allin, Jr., et al. vs. Tennessee Valley Presbytery [ M6GA (1978): — Summary: Alleged errors of the Presbytery in handling a complaint from members of a Session.
title: Luther L. Allin, Jr., et al. vs. Tennessee Valley Presbytery [ M6GA (1978): — Summary: Alleged errors of the Presbytery in handling a complaint from members of a Session.
synopsis: Luther L. Allin, Jr., and other members of the Session of Westminster Presbyterian Church, Chattanooga, Tennessee, complained against Tennessee Valley Presbytery for improper handling of a complaint about the Session's refusal to call a congregational meeting. The Judicial Commission sustained errors regarding inadequate notice (less than 10 days), improper participation of Wallace Bristow and Pastor Vitullo as voting members with direct personal interest, and remanded the matter to Presbytery for proper rehearing.

The SJC sustained the complaint by reference to its decision in Case 2008-15, Issue 5, holding that Presbytery erred in appointing the Sealy Commission in the first place since the underlying August 7 congregational meeting was illegitimate.

title: David Lockwood, Jr. and Evan Holyfield vs. Central Carolina Presbytery [ M11GA (1983): 152-154] — Summary: (See also Cases 27, 29, 33, 36 and the Appendage after Case 36)
synopsis: Complaint by David Lockwood, Jr. and Evan Holyfield against the Central Carolina Presbytery's judicial commission for allegedly failing to act on their May 1982 complaint and charging the pastor and session of New Life Presbyterian Church with improper church discipline procedures, lack of reconciliation efforts, and removal of communing members without due process. The judicial commission determined that items 1-2 (that the commission failed to act) were not sustained based on 23 documented meetings addressing these matters; item 3 was not sustained, finding that the 'determined minority' did not comply with the commission's reconciliation plan; and items 4-5 were not sustained.

title: Session of Harvestwood PCA vs. New River Presbytery (Complaint) p. 144
synopsis: The SJC sustained the complaint of the Session of Harvestwood Presbyterian Church against New River Presbytery, holding that Presbytery erred in ruling on the acceptability of a Sunday school teacher without having the matter properly referred for final disposition or through appeal/complaint channels, and without an adequate record of what the teacher actually believed regarding theological matters such as theistic evolution and the historicity of Adam.

synopsis: The Session of Trinity Presbyterian Church, Slidell, Louisiana complained that Grace Presbytery violated proper procedure when it delayed the ordination of Adrian Pater, a licentiate who had been examined and sustained for ordination as an evangelist. The Judicial Commission found that inadequate minutes were presented and returned the matter to Grace Presbytery for a new hearing, while recommending that the General Assembly affirm that the BCO does not envision ordination expressly to pastoral services in churches of other denominations.

canonical_title: Linton v. Western Carolina Presbytery
title: Linton v. Western Carolina Presbytery
synopsis: The Standing Judicial Commission affirmed that Presbytery of Western Carolina did not err in conducting its BCO 31-2 investigation of accusations against TE Craig Bulkeley or in finding no strong presumption of guilt. The SJC found no constitutional error in Presbytery's procedures and gave great deference to the lower court's discretionary judgment, noting that BCO 31-2 does not mandate specific procedures for investigations. The SJC also held that complainants Kirk Lyons and Robert Woodward lacked standing to bring complaints under BCO 43-1 and 11-4.

title: Mr. & Mrs. Matthew Bernard vs. Ascension Presbytery

The Standing Judicial Commission determined the appeal was administratively out of order because it had not been heard by the next higher court (Ascension Presbytery) before being brought to the General Assembly. The Commission held that appeals must go to the next higher court, not the court of original jurisdiction.

title: Appeal of Chastain v. Heritage — M35GA, 2007 Memphis, p. 82. JOO 15-3. C-Op. D-Op 3. Obj. Summary: Out of order because Appellant renounced PCA jurisdiction on May 8, 2006. "Therefore, all proceedings after May 8, 2006, in this matter are moot, and the decision in SJC 2005-1 remains in effect."
synopsis: Appeal of TE Michael Chastain v. Heritage Presbytery. Chastain challenged Heritage Presbytery's judgment of guilty on charges of divisive behavior and censure of indefinite suspension from office and sacraments. The SJC sustained Presbytery's judgment, finding that Chastain's public letters to the congregation expressing disagreement with Session decisions violated his ordination vows to be subject to his brethren and maintain the peace and unity of the Church, even though his underlying views were not sinful.

title: Urish v. Rocky Mountain Presbytery — M38GA, 2010 Nashville, p. 235. Not sustained 20-0. C-Op 3. Summary: Alleged P erred by ordaining a man who held that women could, under Session authority, do any teaching in a church other than preaching. (Interpretation of 1 Timothy 2:12) Issue: Did Rocky Mounta
synopsis: Rocky Mountain Presbytery sustained a candidate's ordination exam who held the view that women could teach (but not preach or exercise authority) in church ministries under the authority of the church session. TE James Urish complained that Presbytery erred in sustaining the candidate's exam. The SJC affirmed Presbytery's decision, finding no clear error, as neither the Westminster Standards nor the BCO explicitly address this specific application of 1 Timothy 2:12.

Appeal alleged seven specifications of error. SJC sustained convictions but vacated suspension and dissolution of call and
title: Appeal TE John Grady vs. Southwest Florida Presbytery
synopsis: The Standing Judicial Commission heard an appeal by TE John Grady from his conviction and censure by Southwest Florida Presbytery. The Commission sustained three of seven error specifications but found only one materially prejudicial. Critically, the Commission found that Presbytery misapplied BCO 13-9.c by claiming power to direct the minister to resign and then censuring him for refusal to comply; the Commission vacated the censure and remanded for reconsideration of appropriate punishment while the administrative suspension under BCO 31-10 remained in effect.

canonical_title: Lyons v. Western Carolina Presbytery
title: Lyons v. Western Carolina Presbytery
synopsis: Kirk Lyons and others complained that Western Carolina Presbytery erred when it did not find a strong presumption of guilt against TE Craig Bulkeley in a 31-2 investigation, and violated due process by failing to interview the accusing parties. The Standing Judicial Commission sustained the complaint in part, finding that the Presbytery had denied the complainants due process by not allowing them to present evidence before finding no strong presumption of guilt.

Sustained 18-1 Summary: Alleged P erred when it sustained a C alleging, (1) S erred by adding items to docket of congregational meeting and (2) S erred by recommending a course of action to congrega
title: Session of First Presbyterian Augusta v. Savannah River Presbytery — M33GA, 2005 Chattanooga, p. 138. Sustained 18-1 Summary: Alleged P erred when it sustained a C alleging, (1) S erred by adding items to docket of congregational meeting and (2) S erred by recommending a course of action to congrega
synopsis: The Standing Judicial Commission sustained the complaint of the Session of First Presbyterian Church, Augusta, finding that Savannah River Presbytery erred in annulling the Session's actions to dissolve the official relationship with eleven ruling elders. The SJC held that BCO 25-2 does not prohibit a Session from adding agenda items to congregational meetings called pursuant to petitions, and that BCO 24-6 does not prevent a Session from informing the congregation of this right and recommending its exercise.

Summary: Alleged P erred in its Commission's opinion regarding an RE's views related to race and IQ in an email. Issue: Did Presbytery err in its Commission's opinion regarding the views of Mr. Payne, spec
title: Hutchinson & Bulkeley v. Western Carolina — M38GA, 2010 Nashville, p. 178. Not sustained 21-0. Summary: Alleged P erred in its Commission's opinion regarding an RE's views related to race and IQ in an email. Issue: Did Presbytery err in its Commission's opinion regarding the views of Mr. Payne, spec
synopsis: Complaint by TEs Hutchinson and Bulkeley against Western Carolina Presbytery's Inman Commission opinion regarding views of RE Payne expressed in a November 26, 2007 e-mail. The SJC found that Presbytery did not err because it would be inappropriate for the SJC to render judgment on the Biblical character of a man's views expressed in an e-mail without a more complete statement of those views and proper due process.

title: TE David H. Bryson, et al. vs. Central Georgia Presbytery [M10GA (1982): — Summary: Session closing a disciplinary matter in a case of unbiblical divorce and remarriage prior to judicial action by Session.
synopsis: The judicial commission sustained the complaints of David H. Bryson et al. against the Presbytery of Central Georgia for failing to sustain a complaint against the Session of Liberty Presbyterian Church for closing a disciplinary matter involving unbiblical marital separation, divorce, and remarriage. The commission cited biblical teaching on marriage dissolution and the nature of repentance from the Westminster Confession of Faith and catechisms.

synopsis: Complaint of TE Sang Chul Choi against Korean Central Presbytery. Case was found to be out of order on procedural grounds and was not substantively decided by the Standing Judicial Commission.

title: Yuan: An appeal to the SJC alleging a "minister's heresy"

canonical_title: Jones v. Louisiana. [ M36GA (2008): 113]. Sustained 22-0 — Summary: Alleged P erred in not finding a strong presumption of guilt RE TE Wilkins. Jones was joined by seven other complainants. Issues: 1. Did Louisiana Presbytery fail to apply the correct Constitutional standard when it sought to determ
title: Jones v. Louisiana. [ M36GA (2008): 113]. Sustained 22-0 — Summary: Alleged P erred in not finding a strong presumption of guilt RE TE Wilkins. Jones was joined by seven other complainants. Issues: 1. Did Louisiana Presbytery fail to apply the correct Constitutional standard when it sought to determ
synopsis: The Standing Judicial Commission sustained the complaint of TE Jones et al. against Louisiana Presbytery, finding that LAP erred in two ways: first, in failing to apply the correct constitutional standard for determining whether TE Wilkins had differences with the Westminster Standards (differences need not involve overt contradiction or denial, but can arise from different interpretations of wording or biblical exegesis); and second, in failing to adequately investigate and evaluate such differences as required by RAO 16-3(e)(5), thereby neglecting its duty to condemn erroneous opinions that injure the peace or purity of the church.

canonical_title: Blevins v. Westminster — M33GA, 2005 Chattanooga, p. 131. Sustained 20-0. C-Op Summary: Alleged P erred when it ruled a C was not timely filed that related to S denial of request for a new trial asserting "new testimony." BCO 35-13, BCO 43-1, 43-2 Issue: Did Presbytery err in its commission's ruling
title: Blevins v. Westminster — M33GA, 2005 Chattanooga, p. 131. Sustained 20-0. C-Op Summary: Alleged P erred when it ruled a C was not timely filed that related to S denial of request for a new trial asserting "new testimony." BCO 35-13, BCO 43-1, 43-2 Issue: Did Presbytery err in its commission's ruling
synopsis: The Standing Judicial Commission ruled that the Blevins/Seufert complaint filed in June 2003 against Westminster Presbytery's Session was timely, holding that when new testimony surfaces (Presbytery's February 2003 ruling recognizing adultery as biblical grounds for divorce), a new 30-day window opens for filing complaints about the court's new judgment on that evidence. Westminster Presbytery erred in dismissing the complaint as untimely.

canonical_title: Session of Korean Presbyterian Church of Washington v. Korean Capital
title: Session of Korean Presbyterian Church of Washington v. Korean Capital
synopsis: Complaint of Session of Korean Presbyterian Church of Washington vs. Korean Capital Presbytery was found to be prematurely filed because the Presbytery had not completed its action on the complaint. The Standing Judicial Commission dismissed the case as not yet ripe for judicial review.

canonical_title: TE Carl W. Bogue, et al. vs. Ascension Presbytery [ M8GA (1980): — Summary: Ordination of a candidate, who held to continued revelation, and also who did not have a proper call.
title: TE Carl W. Bogue, et al. vs. Ascension Presbytery [ M8GA (1980): — Summary: Ordination of a candidate, who held to continued revelation, and also who did not have a proper call.
synopsis: Carl W. Bogue et al. complained against the Presbytery of the Ascension's sustaining of ordination trials for Mr. Sam Serio and approval of his call to a campus ministry. The Judicial Commission sustained both counts of the complaint, finding that the Presbytery failed to adequately protect fundamental teaching of the WCF and BCO concerning the cessation of extraordinary gifts of the Spirit and the finality of revelation in Scripture, and failed to secure a proper written call that conformed to BCO 21-1. The Commission remanded the matter for re-examination of Serio and securing a proper call.

title: James Landrum vs. Mississippi Valley Presbytery 26-67, p 222
synopsis: Complainants challenged Mississippi Valley Presbytery's approval of James Blaha's theological examination for ordination regarding his views on charismatic gifts. The Special Judicial Commission upheld the challenge regarding prophecy (finding Blaha's views allow possibility of continuing revelation contrary to WCF 1.1 and 6), but rejected the challenge regarding tongues (finding private prayer language use within bounds of 1974 Pastoral Letter). Commission ordered presbytery to note prophecy exception in minutes and instruct Blaha not to teach or preach this view.

canonical_title: TE W. Ted Smith, Jr. vs. Central Carolina Presbytery [ M11GA (1983): 155-157] — Summary: (See also Cases 27,29, 31, 33) Was Presbytery negligent concerning General Assembly directives and BCO responsibilities to exercise proper oversight of past and present members of a congregation.
title: TE W. Ted Smith, Jr. vs. Central Carolina Presbytery [ M11GA (1983): 155-157] — Summary: (See also Cases 27,29, 31, 33) Was Presbytery negligent concerning General Assembly directives and BCO responsibilities to exercise proper oversight of past and present members of a congregation.
synopsis: Complaint of W. Ted Smith, Jr. against Central Carolina Presbytery regarding the presbytery's alleged negligence in exercising proper oversight of New Life Presbyterian Church and its members. The complaint arose from disputes over member dismissals and the presbytery's handling of disciplinary procedures. The General Assembly Commission judged the complaint not sustained as it appeared more as a protest than a formal complaint, though the substance of the issues was addressed in other related cases.

synopsis: Western Carolina Presbytery and two other presbyteries requested the General Assembly to assume original jurisdiction over a teaching elder regarding allegations that women preachers filled the pulpit at Cedar Springs Presbyterian Church. The Standing Judicial Commission dismissed the matter as not properly before the General Assembly, ruling that the conduct of worship services is under the jurisdiction of the Session, not subject to process against a minister under BCO 34-1, and that Tennessee Valley Presbytery did act on the allegations.

title: Appeal of Sang Roo Ryoo v. Korean Capital — M30GA, 2002 Birmingham, p. 124. Sustained 19-0 Summary: Alleged P commission did not follow BCO procedures in judicial process. Remanded for new trial in compliance w/ BCO 32. Issue: Did the Judicial Commission follow BCO procedures in instituting and cond
synopsis: Appeal of suspension from ministry. TE Sang Soo Ryoo appealed his suspension imposed by Korean Capital Presbytery's commission on charges of heresy and schism. The SJC found that Presbyterian judicial procedures were not followed as required by BCO 32, particularly regarding proper service of indictment and notice. The case was therefore remanded to Presbytery for a new trial in compliance with BCO 32.

canonical_title: Appeal of RE Robar v. Central Carolina
title: Appeal of RE Robar v. Central Carolina
synopsis: RE Scott Robar appealed his conviction by Central Carolina Presbytery but filed notice of appeal only with the presbytery's stated clerk, not with the General Assembly's stated clerk as required by BCO 42-4. The SJC found the appeal judicially out of order due to improper filing. A dissenting opinion argued for equitable treatment where the presbytery's own stated clerk had given Robar incorrect advice, citing the Plowman precedent.

canonical_title: Ball v. Westminster — M30GA, 2002 Birmingham, p. 162. Not sustained 17-1. Summary: TE alleged P erred when it adopted a plan to leave the PCA at a future date. Issue: Did P err when it denied the complaint of Larry Ball against the decision of P to withdraw from the PCA pursuant to procedures adopte
title: Complaint TE Larry Ball vs. Westminster Presbytery
synopsis: TE Larry Ball complained that Westminster Presbytery erred in approving a plan to withdraw from the PCA. The SJC affirmed that a presbytery has the constitutional right to withdraw from the PCA as part of historic American grass-roots Presbyterianism, subject to proper procedures. The court held that the relationship between General Assembly and presbytery mirrors that between presbytery and local church, protecting each level's distinct identity and civil operations.

synopsis: Complaint of the 'Determined Minority' of New Life Presbyterian Church, Charlotte, NC against the Judicial Commission of Central Carolina Presbytery regarding improper dismissal of church members. title: Appeal of Fitzsimmons v. Evangel Presbytery [ M26GA (998): 131]. synopsis: The Standing Judicial Commission sustained an appeal by E. L. Fitzsimmons against Evangel Presbytery's handling of his appeal from excommunication. The court found that Presbytery erred by failing to notify the appellant of the time, date, and place for hearing the appeal and by rescinding its earlier finding that the appeal was in order, thus failing to adjudicate the appeal properly. The case was remanded to Evangel Presbytery for proper adjudication on the merits.

title: Hutchinson & Bulkeley v. Western Carolina — M38GA, 2010 Nashville, p. 174. Not sustained 21-0. Summary: Alleged P erred by not suspending RE from sacraments in addition to imposing indefinite suspension from office (in a BCO 38-1 case without process). Issue: Did Presbytery err at its stated meeting
synopsis: Complaint by TEs Hutchinson and Bulkeley against Western Carolina Presbytery, alleging that Presbytery erred by not suspending ruling elder RE Payne from the Sacraments when imposing indefinite suspension from office for scandalous conduct. The SJC sustained the Presbytery's decision, finding no error in constitutional interpretation and deferring to Presbytery's discretionary judgment regarding appropriate censure.

canonical_title: Lachman v. Philadelphia
title: Lachman v. Philadelphia
synopsis: The Standing Judicial Commission held that Philadelphia Presbytery did not act unconstitutionally in appointing a commission to hear a memorial from RE David Lachman and adopting its report. The Commission found no evidence of gross unconstitutional proceedings by the Presbytery or its subordinate commission, and upheld the Presbytery's procedures for handling memorials under BCO 40-5.

canonical_title: Appeal of Choon Soon Lee v. Korean Capital — M30GA, 2002 Birmingham, p. 149. Sustained 15-1 Summary: Alleged P commission did not have jurisdiction to conduct trial of an RE. Issues: Was P and/or its Commission appointed on January 8, 2001 the proper court to assume original jurisdiction to receive
title: Appeal RE Choon Soon Lee vs. Korean Capital Presbytery
synopsis: The Standing Judicial Commission reversed the Korean Capital Presbytery's action in adjudicating charges against RE Choon Soon Lee. The court held that a ruling elder member of a local congregation may only be subject to judicial process originating in the Session of that church, not in the Presbytery, as the Session has original jurisdiction over its members.

canonical_title: Grady v. Southwest Florida — M36GA, 2008 Dallas, p. 125. Sustained 12-2. C-Op. Summary: SJC ruled P erred by including in its Minutes a Committee report with a finding of sins and errors of a TE. SJC ruled that action essentially imposed censure of Admonition without due process. Issue: Did Southwes
title: Complaint of TE John Grady vs. Southwest Florida Presbytery
synopsis: Southwest Florida Presbytery erred when it read into its May 8, 2007 minutes the Shepherding Committee's report containing findings of sins and errors against TE Grady without due process, effectively imposing the censure of admonition without proper judicial procedure. The SJC sustained the complaint and directed the presbytery to expunge the Shepherding Committee report from its minutes.

canonical_title: Judicial Reference from Evangel
title: Judicial Reference from Evangel Presbytery for Adjudication
synopsis: Evangel Presbytery referred a matter to the Standing Judicial Commission for adjudication (Case 2002-04). The SJC did not accede to the request to accept and act upon the judicial reference.

canonical_title: Nichols & Couch v. James River
title: Nichols & Couch v. James River
synopsis: Judicial Case 2002-03 consolidated the complaints of Ruling Elders Leland Nichols and Sam Couch against James River Presbytery's handling of West End Presbyterian Church's constitutional request to dissolve the pastoral relationship with TE Robert Wilson. The SJC found that James River Presbytery had no constitutional basis to delay granting the congregation's proper constitutional request to dissolve the pastoral relation and sustained the complainants' arguments on the central issue, while finding some complaints judicially out of order and upholding the presbytery's discretion regarding benevolent gift-giving.

title: Session, BPC, Concord, NC vs. Central Carolina Presbytery (Complaint) p. 148
synopsis: Complaint against Central Carolina Presbytery was not sustained by a vote of 11-7. Two dissenting opinions were recorded.

canonical_title: Hopper v. James River Presbytery
title: Hopper v. James River Presbytery
topics: extraordinary gifts of the Holy Spirit
peace and purity of the church
synopsis: Robert D. Hopper, pastor of Grace Covenant Church in Williamsburg, Virginia, challenged James River Presbytery's April 1991 action forbidding him from preaching and teaching views affirming that the extraordinary gifts of the Holy Spirit (including prophecy and tongues) may be present in the church in the present age. The Standing Judicial Commission denied the complaint, holding that presbytery has constitutional authority under BCO 13-9 and 34-5 to restrict teaching and preaching when it determines such views may be harmful to the spiritual welfare of the churches under its care, and that such restriction does not violate liberty of conscience absent a determination that the forbidden teaching is heretical.
